speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 16, 1995. The survivor is Felix Pierson, P-I-E-R-S-O-N. The interviewer is Ruth Resnikoff, R-E-S-N-I-K-O-F-F. The interview is in Del Ray Beach in the Miami, Florida, USA region. The language will be English and this will be roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 16, 1995. I would like to introduce Mr. Felix Pierson, P-I-E-R-S-O-N. And I'm Ruth Resnikoff, R-E-S-N-I-K-O-F-F. And we're at the Piersons' lovely home in Del Ray Beach, Florida. And this is tape one. Could you tell us your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Felix Pierson, P, as in pole, I-E-R-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my original name was P-I-E-R-N-I-K-A-R-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Piernikarz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Felix. It used to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you had another name--

speaker: interviewee
text: Feivel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feivel. And you had a nickname, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everybody called me Fafzha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 17, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city of Lask, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-A-S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the population of Lask?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was between 5,000 and 6,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jews and how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: About 50% were Jews. It was a real Jewish town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was three years younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. From my mother's side, I had a grandfather and a grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was Liebish Mendel, and my grandmother's name was-- we called her Frada, It's really Frieda. And she was-- my grandmother was the head of the family. But it's interesting that I mostly remember my grandfather, the little things that he told me, and the little sayings that he had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He used to say-- he used to say-- there were the Polish kids always chasing us, and trying to beat us up-- or they thought they could. But our parents told us not to fight them. So my grandfather used to teach us that if a dog chases you, and you run away, the dog will chase you more. But if you stop, so will the dog. And you know, it proved right. The minute we stood up to these Polish hooligans, they stopped chasing us. So he was-- he was, in a quiet way, a very bright, very smart man with many things that, you know, I remember him quite often. He was very proud of me. You know, he was not a generous man with words. Like today, we tell our grandchildren how much we love them three times a day. My grandfather loved me just the same as I love our grandchildren. But I remember one time in particular, I was the dressed in a nice high school uniform. I didn't go to high school, but my father was a tailor. So he dressed me a little bit in advance. And I carried the flag-- the Polish flag going to the synagogue on Polish Independence Day. And we passed my grandfather's house. And on the way back, he called me in and gave me 20 grosze. And that was a lot of money. And I knew it was an expression of love, the only way my grandfather could. He couldn't say, I love you. But that-- that was-- excuse me-- and I knew what he meant. He meant to say that I love you. I'm proud of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a lovely memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And on my father's side, I only had a grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She wasn't, what we would say, comfortable. She was a woman that-- she was very old. To me, that time, she looked very old, but she had to be anywhere between 70 and 80, or closer to 80. And yet, she was sitting in the marketplace, as I will show you a picture that we have in [NON-ENGLISH] album, always murmuring or memorizing a prayer while she was selling her vegetables in the marketplace. In the synagogue, she was what is called in Yiddish, [YIDDISH], that she would read the prayers and other women that couldn't read the Hebrew would repeat after her. And the humorous side that the children always would watch her doing, is she would sit by her window Saturday afternoon and read her [NON-ENGLISH]. But actually what she did was watching if any Jewish person doesn't go in the movie house on Saturday afternoon. And she would be very angry at those people that would, because she felt that that means you're committing a sin-- was heresy-- and she's going to suffer on account of that. She was a righteous orthodox person. While she was very poor, she lived mostly from what my father used to help her. But her being on the marketplace gave her that feeling of independence that she is earning some money. And she was shot on the way, when we were deported from our hometown. When we were chased from the houses to the church, she couldn't walk that fast. So a German pulled out a gun and shot her on the-- on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see it. I didn't see it. But there were 65 people from my hometown that were shot by Germans, by the people that chased us out from our home. And there was a common grave on the cemetery. There were people that were right after the war, friends of my relatives-- one relative that was-- has a picture of the grave-- of the mass grave of the 65 people. While I was later on in Poland, there was no sign of that grave. No sign of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to your childhood before those things began to happen, can you tell me about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- it's funny that I very seldom talk about my mother. It's always my father. My mother was a real devoted mother, as mothers could be. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was a quiet person, didn't talk much. But during the war years, she gave me two deutsche mark in my pocket. At that time, I probably was 14 years old. She knew I was going on a date to spend $2, 2 deutsche mark on the girl. There was no place to go-- you couldn't spend it anywhere. But she knew that it's nice if a boy has a few-- a little bit money in his pocket. Wasn't a date, it was just a walk in the street. But she was a very devoted person. Her children were her life. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I never talked about it. That is so painful. That is so painful, because most of the people of my time, when we meet, they always talk about my father, what a wonderful person he was. And he was. When he walked into our house, the house would light up. He would sing, he would entertain. He played on the stage, an amateur group. The money that came in, they would use for poor brides for a dowry, for poor people for winter, for coal or for matches. He belonged what is called the [NON-ENGLISH], which actually is like a credit union where they would loan money to people that couldn't afford to pay interest, and they didn't pay any interest. He was in the city-- in the town that we lived in, he was on the board from the Red Cross. He was an officer in the Polish army. He was so involved in so many-- he was a devoted Zionist. He was-- And everyone, people older than me that were his friends, that I have met after the war, certain individuals from Belgium or from the United States, they talk about my father like some-- you know, the think that they're doing me a favor by talking so well about my father. But really, I was so deeply hurt. Every time I heard them talking about him, my heart was going out for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Especially, you know, the way I lost him. What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a tailor. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We employed a few people and it was considered-- at those times, they were considered comfortable. You know, my mother with the children would travel in a-- into the country, which was like a few kilometers away from our house. Where we lived was the country, but it was-- but for a few months we would go to the country so we could inhale some better-- better air.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your house? I know you showed me a picture. But did it have electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. We had electricity and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have running water, but the pump was right outside of the yard that I showed you in the picture. And it was an electrical pump that accumulated the water, and all we did is this pull the handle and it would fill up the water. It was a modern building. The owner of the building-- and I had mentioned to you he was one of the richest people in town. They didn't have the water come up, but they had it connected where the-- they could pour water downstairs to it. That they put in an extended pipe. That was already, you know, sort of a modern house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talking about water, if you want to have a drink, Felix.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for refrigeration for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a basement where it was always cool. And we kept stuff that needed to be refrigerated. Somehow we didn't need so much to be refrigerated. But we kept [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things in the basement to be refrigerated. I remember, I loved to go down to the basement-- the cellar really, it wasn't a basement, it was a cellar-- because if my mother would ask me to bring up some cherries that she had put into a jar. That made wine, but the cherries we had for dessert. So I would go down in the basement and have a sip of that wine. So I felt grown up that I, you know, steal that sort of a sip of wine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, we spoke Yiddish. But of course, we went to a Polish school and we spoke perfect Polish, my father being an officer in the Polish army. And we had many-- rather most-- Gentile customers, so we would speak-- my father would speak Polish. My mother also. Of course, she went to school. Everyone went to school, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: On holidays and Shabbat and so on, do remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Every Friday night, we would go to a Hevrah. In this small town, we had a large synagogue, a beautiful, beautiful synagogue that the Germans had turned into-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what did they turn it into? They kept the horses there-- a barn. And the Poles have now a firehouse made out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had this beautiful, tremendous synagogue where my grandfather would pray. But my father-- And then there was a [NON-ENGLISH], Hasidic [NON-ENGLISH], and it was Alexander, and I think there was another one. And my father belonged to a Hevrah, that was mostly partisans, you know? Artisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Artisans. I keep thinking-- artisans, were, you know, tailors, and shoemakers, and other people like that. And my father would pray before the congregation. That's called a [NON-ENGLISH], but I think he liked to be called a [NON-ENGLISH]. He sang beautiful songs, beautiful melodies. Modern melodies, but older prayers. And it was every Friday night, we would go to that synagogue and bring home a guest, that was called a [NON-ENGLISH] for dinner. There wasn't a Friday that my father wouldn't take home someone, you know, that would come to the city as-- those people that are wandering from town to town, they stopped for Shabbat. They wouldn't just-- And that time, Friday night, people would take home to their houses for dinner. And we always had a place for an [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your schooling. You went to a school that was just for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there were-- there were two public schools in our town. There was-- one was called Public School Number One, where Gentile children would go. And there was the Jewish public school, which was in a dilapidated building. The Gentile children had a beautiful, four-storey brick building. But since there were 50% of Jewish children in our time, and a higher percentage of Jewish children would go to school, so some of the children had to attend-- that-- had to go to-- had to go to school in that new building. But the courses were taught the same as they were in the Jewish school. And during recess, if we would come up on the steps a little bit later than the-- the whistle would blow to come up, those Jewish boys or girls that would be caught on the stairs the Polish boys would always beat up, always and never miss. So it-- so we started to go up in groups. You know, because at least in groups, they wouldn't fight us. But this is the way we grew up. We always had to fight off, you know, the Polish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you told me you were a member of two organizations, Jewish organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It must have been more than two, but I remember the [NON-ENGLISH] I belonged for a long time. And for a short time, we tried to be grown up and independent, and we joined the left wing Zionist organization, Hashomer Hatzair. And that was a very short-- it lasted a very short time, because one day when we were out for a walk with the organization, by the time we came back the police-- our parents have brought the police to lock up that left-- Zionist organization. So--but talking about schooling, we also went to a Hebrew school. First it was a cheder, which was called Torah Bada'at, which is Torah and knowledge. But that lasted only a few years, because I remember shortly before the war a teacher came to our town that taught us Sephardic-- that's the way they spoke in Israel-- and it was a much, much more modern Hebrew school, the way we call it, [NON-ENGLISH]. And those traditional orthodox people panicked when they saw this thing happening, but it was very popular because everyone looked ahead to be-- to learn the language, to learn about Palestine, and one day to be able to emigrate to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the end of the war, that was what's called a [NON-ENGLISH], but literally translated it's-- [NON-ENGLISH] is grammar. But it meant much more. [NON-ENGLISH] combined education and knowledge and grammar and everything. And we considered ourselves much more modern, much more worldly than those that would go to a cheder. During this period, Hitler was rising in power in Germany. Were you aware of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we were aware towards the later-- late '30s, when people started to come to our town that were thrown out of Germany because they were born in Poland. Prior to that, we would read in the paper stories that-- Friday night, after we would come from the synagogue, and the father would read the newspaper. And at first, it was have to believe, that the Germans would do things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you reading in the newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: That people are disappearing. That families, in order to get the ashes of the-- of those people that disappeared, they have to pay a ransom. And my father kept saying, how can this be? It's the Germans that were here in World War I. If they saw an older person carrying a sack of coal or potatoes, they would take it from their hand and bring it to their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is happening? Maybe this will pass. Maybe it's not true. Maybe whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard to believe and we were just hoping it'll go away. But it didn't. It didn't go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio? Did you hear Hitler's speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I didn't, but we had a radio and my father would sit late at night to listen all these speeches. And I-- I remember-- I remember that he was very upset. But when the war broke out on September 31--

speaker: interviewer
text: September 1.

speaker: interviewee
text: September 1, of course. The same morning, an aunt of mine had died. And my father said, she must have been a saint, because she's going to avoid everything that's going to happen, that God took her away, she shouldn't suffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had an idea of what was coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it seems that as things were progressing, things were happening that we children weren't told about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just 13 in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, I had bar mitzvah. My father always kept hoping-- every Friday night, he would say, now that our-- our oldest son is getting bar mitzvah, in another three years, our younger son will be bar mitzvah, and will be able to say grace [NON-ENGLISH], which means you have to have a quorum of three to be able to say grace. But, of course, you could say it with strangers, but my father was-- couldn't wait for the minute we would have it in our family, and he never lived-- he never lived to this moment. I remember when, on August 30-- no, August 31, it was a Thursday-- we went from HaNoar HaTzioni, and had a thing, what was called a scout war, where you go into the woods, two groups that are wearing different colored scarf ties. And the purpose is if you tap someone-- if you touch the shoulder-- he's got to give you his tie. It was that kind of war. And we came back and it was already little dark, but we saw on the walls the sign, mobilization. And we were so happy we're were going to see a real war. Children, you know, are excited they're going to see a real war. The very next day, September 1, there were already dead people in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dead people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, because we were pretty close-- pretty close to the German border. And soldiers, or individuals, would walk on the bridge and planes would come down. And you know, just-- they were looking-- the German planes were flying to see-- what are these called? The hunter planes. And they saw people on the bridge, so they shot them. I don't know if they were Jewish or not Jewish. But that was-- and on that the following Sunday-- the following Sunday, which was September the 3rd, we were-- the sirens were, you know, were on, because it was an alarm that the German planes are coming on. So we were hiding in the cornfield, small cornfield. And a stupid Polish policeman picked up a rifle and tried to shoot with a rifle to the plane. Immediately, these planes swarmed down on that spot, and they started throwing bombs. And at that time, there were 39 people. Some were killed, some were, you know, injured, very seriously. Because I remember my father was working on them under the-- by the pump, because we were washing them up. That was all so primitive at that time. Everyone was completely unprepared for this situation. And-- This is-- this is how it started. It was within a week's time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the first anti-Jewish actions that you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they come-- came to-- before they came to town, I and my father ran away from town, thinking that we will run away from the Germans, because we were men. We were afraid they were going to hurt us. So by the time we walked to the next town, which was 18 kilometers, like 12 miles, they were already there. And when we walked back, we saw dead people lined up, you know, in the- in the ditches and along the road. So when we came to the house-- to the house, my mother somehow expected that we were coming back, because she had prepared for us a Shabbat meal. It was a Friday. But the first anti-Jewish-- it was really at first, it started with the intelligentsia of the town. They would take the teachers, the priests, the rabbi-- any people that the [NON-ENGLISH] in town, that the Volksdeutschen would tell them, that were patriots, Polish patriots. And there were some Jewish people that were very dedicated Polish citizens, so they took everyone up. It wasn't exactly Jews. They took out people in the marketplace and they gave them brooms to sweep the dirt and the mud. This is something like you're pushing water. There's dirt in the marketplace. There's dirt on-- on-- on the cobblestone. And then they started the beatings in the street and they started to take the people, grab people from the street to work. And in my case, they took me to clean latrines by hand. You know, toilets to clean with the hands, not-- they didn't even give you a shovel. Everything was so shocking that you couldn't comprehend what was-- what was happening.

words.text: they didn't even give you a

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a cloth or something to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing, nothing. But everything started gradually. Everything started gradually, because the goal was to dehumanize us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Humiliate, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not humiliate. Dehumanize. Yes, that was their call. That was their purpose. And I think in the long run, they did it to themselves, but we were the ones that suffered physically. And this was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go on on the next tape.
